title: The Joke That Had to Be Betrayed
summary: A literary translator and a scripture-society editor argue over whether a translation owes its loyalty to the exact words or to their effect — each holding a truth that breaks the other's rule.

body.speaker: Mr Halvorsen
body.text: Ms Okafor, I've read your draft of the novel, and on page nine you've simply invented a pun. The French original turns on two words that sound alike. Your English turns on two completely different words. That is not translation. That is composition.

body.speaker: Ms Okafor
body.text: It is translation of the joke, which is what the sentence is. If I render the French words literally, the English reader sees a flat sentence and wonders why the other characters are laughing. A joke that lands nowhere is not faithful — it's just accurate, which is a different thing.

body.speaker: Mr Halvorsen
body.text: Then footnote it. Explain the pun. At least the reader knows what was actually said.

body.speaker: Ms Okafor
body.text: And laughs at nothing. A joke explained is a joke killed; you've handed the reader a corpse and a label. My job is to reproduce the effect — the small jolt of delight in the place the original put it. To do that I had to abandon the words. There was no third door.

body.speaker: Mr Halvorsen
body.text: I'll grant you the joke. Honestly, I will — for a joke, the effect is the meaning, and I can't argue with a laugh. But you can't run the whole trade on that principle, and here's where I dig in. My society publishes scripture. If you 'reproduce the effect' of a sacred text by making it read more beautifully than the original, you haven't served it. You've rewritten it under its own name.

body.speaker: Ms Okafor
body.text: That's fair, and it's the strongest thing you've said. There are texts where the words themselves carry authority I have no right to improve. A contract, a scripture, a dying person's instruction. With those, fidelity to the word has to win, even at the cost of the effect.

body.speaker: Mr Halvorsen
body.text: So you concede the rule is 'words first'.

body.speaker: Ms Okafor
body.text: No — I concede there is no single rule, which is the opposite. You want one master; I'm telling you the translator serves two, and they pull apart in hard cases. Fidelity to the words and fidelity to the effect are not the same loyalty. My skill isn't choosing one forever. It's judging, sentence by sentence, which debt this sentence has to pay first.

body.speaker: Mr Halvorsen
body.text: That sounds like a licence to do whatever you please and call it judgement.

body.speaker: Ms Okafor
body.text: It would, if the judgements were hidden. They're not. Every one of them is defensible, and I'll defend each on its own page. What I won't do is pretend translation is a mechanical transfer — pouring meaning from one jar into another with nothing spilled. Something is always spilled. The honest translator chooses what.

body.speaker: Ms Okafor
body.text: Pretends nothing spilled, and usually that means they didn't notice. The reader sees a smooth surface and trusts it. With the joke, I betrayed the words to keep the laugh. With your scripture, I'd betray the laugh to keep the words. Both are betrayals. The traitor, Mr Halvorsen, is just the only one in the room paying close enough attention to know which betrayal this page requires.

grammarNotes.title: Concession that holds ground ('I'll grant you the joke … But here's where I dig in.')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cả hai nhân vật nhượng bộ rồi phản biện theo lối tranh luận B2: 'I'll grant you the joke … But you can't run the whole trade on that principle, and here's where I dig in' và 'That's fair, and it's the strongest thing you've said.' Khi Halvorsen cố quy kết 'So you concede the rule is words first', Okafor bác lại bằng 'No — I concede there is no single rule, which is the opposite', dùng 'not X but Y' để từ chối bị dồn vào một quy tắc duy nhất.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Both speakers concede then push back, in B2 debate style: 'I'll grant you the joke … But you can't run the whole trade on that principle, and here's where I dig in' and 'That's fair, and it's the strongest thing you've said.' When Halvorsen tries to pin her — 'So you concede the rule is words first' — Okafor refuses with 'No — I concede there is no single rule, which is the opposite,' using contrastive 'not … but …' logic to reject being cornered into one rule.

translationVi:
Ông Halvorsen: Cô Okafor, tôi đã đọc bản nháp cuốn tiểu thuyết của cô, và ở trang chín cô đã đơn giản là bịa ra một câu chơi chữ. Nguyên bản tiếng Pháp dựa vào hai từ nghe giống nhau. Bản tiếng Anh của cô dựa vào hai từ hoàn toàn khác. Đó không phải dịch. Đó là sáng tác.

Cô Okafor: Đó là dịch cái câu đùa, vốn là bản chất của câu văn ấy. Nếu tôi chuyển các từ tiếng Pháp theo nghĩa đen, người đọc tiếng Anh thấy một câu nhạt nhẽo và tự hỏi vì sao các nhân vật khác lại cười. Một câu đùa không 'ăn' vào đâu thì không trung thành — nó chỉ chính xác, vốn là một chuyện khác.

Ông Halvorsen: Vậy thì chú thích nó. Giải thích câu chơi chữ. Ít nhất người đọc biết điều thực sự đã được nói.

Cô Okafor: Và cười vào chỗ trống. Một câu đùa được giải thích là một câu đùa đã chết; ông trao cho người đọc một cái xác và một cái nhãn. Việc của tôi là tái tạo hiệu ứng — cú nảy nhỏ của niềm thích thú ở đúng chỗ câu gốc đặt nó. Để làm thế tôi phải từ bỏ câu chữ. Chẳng có cánh cửa thứ ba nào.

Ông Halvorsen: Tôi nhường cô về câu đùa. Thật lòng, tôi nhường — với một câu đùa, hiệu ứng chính là nghĩa, và tôi không thể tranh cãi với một tiếng cười. Nhưng cô không thể vận hành cả cái nghề trên nguyên tắc đó, và đây là chỗ tôi giữ vững. Hội của tôi xuất bản kinh sách. Nếu cô 'tái tạo hiệu ứng' của một văn bản thiêng bằng cách khiến nó đọc lên hay hơn nguyên bản, cô đã không phục vụ nó. Cô đã viết lại nó dưới chính tên của nó.

Cô Okafor: Điều đó đúng, và là điều mạnh nhất ông vừa nói. Có những văn bản nơi chính câu chữ mang một thẩm quyền mà tôi không có quyền cải thiện. Một hợp đồng, một kinh sách, lời dặn của một người hấp hối. Với những thứ đó, trung thành với câu chữ phải thắng, dù phải trả giá bằng hiệu ứng.

Ông Halvorsen: Vậy cô thừa nhận quy tắc là 'câu chữ trước tiên'.

Cô Okafor: Không — tôi thừa nhận không có một quy tắc duy nhất nào, vốn là điều ngược lại. Ông muốn một ông chủ; tôi đang nói với ông rằng người dịch phục vụ hai, và chúng kéo nhau ra hai phía trong những trường hợp khó. Trung thành với câu chữ và trung thành với hiệu ứng không phải cùng một lòng trung. Kỹ năng của tôi không phải chọn một thứ mãi mãi. Mà là phán đoán, từng câu một, món nợ nào câu này phải trả trước.

Ông Halvorsen: Nghe như một tấm giấy phép muốn làm gì thì làm rồi gọi nó là phán đoán.

Cô Okafor: Sẽ là vậy, nếu các phán đoán bị giấu đi. Chúng không bị giấu. Mỗi cái đều có thể biện minh, và tôi sẽ bảo vệ từng cái trên trang của nó. Cái tôi sẽ không làm là giả vờ dịch là một sự chuyển giao máy móc — rót nghĩa từ bình này sang bình khác mà không sánh ra gì. Luôn có cái gì đó sánh ra. Người dịch trung thực chọn cái gì sẽ mất.

Ông Halvorsen: Còn người dịch không trung thực?

Cô Okafor: Giả vờ chẳng có gì sánh ra, và thường thì nghĩa là họ đã không nhận ra. Người đọc thấy một bề mặt trơn nhẵn và tin nó. Với câu đùa, tôi phản bội câu chữ để giữ tiếng cười. Với kinh sách của ông, tôi sẽ phản bội tiếng cười để giữ câu chữ. Cả hai đều là phản bội. Kẻ phản bội, thưa ông Halvorsen, chỉ là người duy nhất trong phòng chú ý đủ kỹ để biết trang này đòi hỏi sự phản bội nào.

questions.prompt: What is Mr Halvorsen's objection to page nine?
options: Okafor invented a different pun instead of translating the French words — he calls it composition, not translation
questions.explanation: 'you've simply invented a pun … That is not translation. That is composition.'

questions.prompt: Why does Okafor say a literal rendering fails here?
options: The reader sees a flat sentence and wonders why the others are laughing
questions.explanation: 'the English reader sees a flat sentence and wonders why the other characters are laughing.'

questions.prompt: What distinction does Okafor draw between 'faithful' and 'accurate'?
options: A joke that lands nowhere is accurate but not faithful — accuracy and faithfulness are different things
questions.explanation: 'it's just accurate, which is a different thing.'

questions.prompt: Why does Okafor reject Halvorsen's footnote suggestion?
options: A joke explained is a joke killed — the reader laughs at nothing
questions.explanation: 'A joke explained is a joke killed; you've handed the reader a corpse and a label.'

questions.prompt: What does Okafor say her job is, with the joke?
options: To reproduce the effect — the jolt of delight in the place the original put it
To add humour the original lacked
questions.explanation: 'My job is to reproduce the effect — the small jolt of delight.'

questions.prompt: What does Halvorsen concede about the joke?
options: That for a joke, the effect is the meaning, and he can't argue with a laugh
questions.explanation: 'for a joke, the effect is the meaning, and I can't argue with a laugh.'

options: On scripture — reproducing the 'effect' of a sacred text by making it read more beautifully rewrites it under its own name
questions.explanation: 'If you reproduce the effect of a sacred text by making it read more beautifully … You've rewritten it under its own name.'

questions.prompt: Which texts does Okafor agree must put fidelity to the word first?
options: A contract, a scripture, a dying person's instruction
questions.explanation: 'A contract, a scripture, a dying person's instruction … fidelity to the word has to win.'

questions.prompt: When Halvorsen says 'So you concede the rule is words first', how does Okafor respond?
options: She says no — she concedes there is no single rule, which is the opposite
questions.explanation: 'No — I concede there is no single rule, which is the opposite.'

questions.prompt: What does Okafor say the translator serves?
options: Two masters — fidelity to the words and to the effect — which pull apart in hard cases
questions.explanation: 'the translator serves two, and they pull apart in hard cases.'

questions.prompt: What does Okafor say her real skill is?
options: Judging, sentence by sentence, which debt that sentence must pay first
questions.explanation: 'judging, sentence by sentence, which debt this sentence has to pay first.'

questions.prompt: How does Okafor answer the charge that judgement is just 'a licence to do whatever you please'?
options: She says it would be, if the judgements were hidden — but they are not, and each is defensible on its own page
questions.explanation: 'It would, if the judgements were hidden. They're not … each on its own page.'

questions.prompt: What metaphor does Okafor reject for translation?
options: A mechanical transfer — pouring meaning from one jar to another with nothing spilled
questions.explanation: 'a mechanical transfer — pouring meaning from one jar into another with nothing spilled.'

options: Halvorsen wants the effect; Okafor wants the words
Halvorsen defends fidelity to the exact words (vital for scripture); Okafor defends fidelity to the effect (vital for a joke) — and each concedes the other's strongest case
questions.explanation: Halvorsen grants the joke; Okafor grants the scripture — then she argues there is no single rule.
questions.hint: Track the concessions on both sides.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Okafor NOT claiming?
options: That the effect should always override the exact words
That accuracy and faithfulness can be different things
That some texts' words carry authority she must not improve
That every translation spills something, and the honest translator chooses what
questions.explanation: 'I concede there is no single rule' — she explicitly denies that the effect always wins.
questions.hint: Find the single fixed rule she rejects in her eighth turn.

questions.prompt: The closing line calls the translator 'the only one in the room paying close enough attention to know which betrayal this page requires.' This suggests:
options: The apparent disloyalty reflects the closest, most trustworthy attention to the text's real demands
questions.explanation: Both options are betrayals; the skilled translator knows which one the page needs.

cloze.template:
Mr Halvorsen: Ms Okafor, on page nine you've simply {{b1}} a pun. The French original turns on two words that sound alike. Your English turns on two completely different words. That is not translation. That is {{b2}}.

Ms Okafor: It is translation of the joke. If I render the French words {{b3}}, the English reader sees a flat sentence and wonders why the others are laughing. A joke that lands nowhere is not faithful — it's just {{b4}}, which is a different thing.

Mr Halvorsen: Then {{b5}} it. Explain the pun.

Ms Okafor: And laughs at nothing. A joke explained is a joke {{b6}}; you've handed the reader a corpse and a label. My job is to reproduce the {{b7}} — the small jolt of delight in the place the original put it. There was no third {{b8}}.

Mr Halvorsen: I'll grant you the joke. But you can't run the whole trade on that principle, and here's where I {{b9}} in. My society publishes scripture. If you make it read more beautifully than the original, you've {{b10}} it under its own name.

Ms Okafor: That's fair. There are texts where the words carry authority I have no right to improve. A contract, a scripture, a dying person's instruction. With those, fidelity to the {{b11}} has to win.

Mr Halvorsen: So you concede the rule is 'words first'.

Ms Okafor: No — I concede there is no single {{b12}}, which is the opposite. The translator serves two, and they pull {{b13}} in hard cases. My skill is judging which debt this sentence has to pay first.

Mr Halvorsen: That sounds like a {{b14}} to do whatever you please.

Ms Okafor: It would, if the judgements were hidden. I won't pretend translation is a mechanical {{b15}} with nothing spilled. Something is always spilled. The honest translator chooses what.

blanks.explanation: 'just accurate, which is a different thing.'
'fidelity to the word has to win.'
'a licence to do whatever you please.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Okafor argues there is no single rule — that faithfulness to the exact words and faithfulness to the effect are two loyalties, and good judgement means knowing which a given sentence must serve first. Think of a time you had to 'translate' something for someone — explaining a technical idea, conveying bad news gently, or passing on someone else's wishes. Did you keep the exact words or recreate the effect? Looking back, did you choose the right master for that moment?
